verdict_note: 12/13 P1 fixed. Remaining P1 (orphan code) is architectural - consumer wiring in next integration task.

findings.title: Message handlers invoked without try/catch or await in ChannelRegistry
findings.description: MessageHandler type signature is `(message: IncomingMessage) => void | Promise<void>`. When a handler returns a Promise (async), that promise is neither awaited nor given a .catch(). If the handler throws synchronously, the error propagates into the message event callback, potentially crashing the Slack/WhatsApp event loop. If async, the rejection is unhandled and silently lost. This is both a fire-and-forget async issue and a missing error boundary around user-supplied callbacks. Additionally, if any handler throws synchronously, it aborts dispatch to subsequent handlers.
findings.suggestion: Wrap each handler call in try/catch and handle async returns: `try { const result = handler(msg); if (result instanceof Promise) result.catch(err => logger.error('Handler error', { platform: msg.platform, error: err })); } catch (err) { logger.error('Sync handler error', { platform: msg.platform, error: err }); }`

findings.title: SlackChannel.connect() has no error handling and never sets 'error' status
findings.description: If app.start() rejects (network error, invalid token, socket timeout), the status remains 'connecting' forever -- it is never set to 'error' or 'disconnected'. The ChannelStatus type includes an 'error' state but neither SlackChannel nor WhatsAppChannel ever transitions to it. When connect() or disconnect() fails, the status remains in 'connecting' or 'connected', which is misleading. Consumers watching status changes get no signal that the channel has entered an error state. The caller gets the raw Bolt error with no channel context.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the connect body in try/catch. On failure, call `this.setStatus('error')` before re-throwing a wrapped error: `throw new Error('Slack connect failed', { cause: error })`. This gives status subscribers accurate state information and enables UI error indicators. Apply the same pattern to WhatsAppChannel.connect() and both disconnect() methods.

findings.title: New package has no consumer -- orphan code
findings.code_snippet:
export { ChannelRegistry } from './channel-registry.js'
export { WhatsAppChannel } from './whatsapp/index.js'
export { SlackChannel } from './slack/index.js'
findings.description: The @proj-airi/channels-extra package exports ChannelRegistry, WhatsAppChannel, and SlackChannel, but no other package.json in the repo declares it as a dependency. The INTEGRATION_PLAN.md references a future `setupExtraChannels(config)` wiring via injeca, but this wiring does not exist in the codebase. There is no import, no entry point, and no handler that reaches this code. The package is dead-on-arrival until a consumer is wired.
findings.suggestion: Add @proj-airi/channels-extra as a dependency in stage-tamagotchi (or the relevant app) and wire it through injeca DI (e.g., in apps/stage-tamagotchi/src/main/services/anima/) with a minimal end-to-end path proving one message can flow through the registry.

findings.title: ChannelRegistry stores all state in memory without persistence
findings.code_snippet:
private entries = new Map<ChannelPlatform, ChannelRegistryEntry>()
private globalHandlers: MessageHandler[] = []
findings.description: The ChannelRegistry maintains the map of registered channels and global message handlers entirely in closure-scoped memory. On process restart, all registrations are lost. Additionally, globalHandlers is an unbounded array -- if consumers register handlers without unsubscribing, it grows without limit. While ChannelPlatform is limited to 2 values so entries is naturally bounded, the globalHandlers array has no cap.
findings.suggestion: 1) For handler accumulation: add a maxHandlers guard or at minimum log a warning when handler count exceeds a threshold. 2) For persistence: if channel registration state needs to survive restarts, persist config to DB/KV and reconstruct on startup. If this is intentionally ephemeral (re-registered on each boot by DI), document that assumption explicitly in a code comment.

findings.title: No logging in any production source file
findings.code_snippet: import type { Channel, ChannelConfig, ChannelPlatform, ChannelRegistryEntry, IncomingMessage, MessageHandler } from './types.js'
findings.description: None of the three production modules (channel-registry.ts, slack/index.ts, whatsapp/index.ts) use @guiiai/logg or any structured logger. Connection lifecycle (connecting/connected/disconnected/error), message receipt, rate limit enforcement, and error paths all lack observability. When a channel fails to connect or a message is silently dropped, there will be zero trace in logs.
findings.suggestion: Add `import { useLogg } from '@guiiai/logg'` and create a logger per module. Log at INFO level for connect/disconnect lifecycle events, WARN for rate limit enforcement, ERROR for connection failures, and DEBUG for message receipt.

findings.title: better-sqlite3 declared as dependency but never imported
findings.code_snippet: "better-sqlite3": "catalog:"
findings.description: The package.json declares better-sqlite3 as a production dependency and @types/better-sqlite3 as a devDependency. However, no source file in the package imports or references SQLite. This inflates the install footprint and misleads consumers about the package's actual requirements.
findings.suggestion: Remove better-sqlite3 from dependencies and @types/better-sqlite3 from devDependencies. If SQLite persistence is planned for channel config storage, add it when that feature is implemented.

findings.title: Slack message handlers invoked without error boundary
findings.description: Same pattern as channel-registry.ts: message handlers (which can be async per the MessageHandler type) are called in a loop without try/catch and without awaiting. A sync throw in handler N aborts handlers N+1..end. An async rejection is silently lost. This runs inside the Bolt message callback, so a sync throw could crash the Bolt event processing.
findings.suggestion: Wrap each handler invocation in try/catch. Await or .catch() any returned Promise. Log errors with context including platform, channelId, senderId, and handler index.

findings.title: WhatsApp message handlers invoked without error boundary
findings.description: Same pattern as Slack channel. MessageHandler can return Promise<void>, but the return value is discarded. A synchronous throw in any handler aborts remaining handlers. An async rejection is silently lost as an unhandled promise rejection. This executes inside a Baileys 'messages.upsert' event handler, so errors could interfere with the Baileys event loop.
findings.suggestion: Wrap each handler invocation in try/catch. Await or .catch() any returned Promise. Log errors with context including platform, remoteJid, and message ID.

findings.title: setStatus() iterates status handlers without error isolation
findings.description: Both SlackChannel (line 46-50) and WhatsAppChannel (line 55-59) iterate over user-supplied status change handlers without try/catch. A throwing handler aborts notification of remaining handlers AND can corrupt the channel's operational flow (e.g., if setStatus('connected') throws midway through handlers, the status IS set to 'connected' but the channel's connect() method may surface the user-handler error instead of completing normally).
findings.suggestion: Wrap each handler call in try/catch: `for (const handler of this.statusHandlers) { try { handler(status) } catch (err) { logger.error('Status handler error', { status, error: err }) } }`

findings.title: onAnyMessage test never verifies message delivery
findings.code_snippet:
const messages: IncomingMessage[] = []
registry.onAnyMessage((msg) => { messages.push(msg) })

expect(messages).toHaveLength(0)
findings.description: The test for onAnyMessage only verifies the handler is subscribed and the array starts empty. It never triggers a message through any registered channel to verify that the global handler actually receives messages. This is a critical behavioral gap -- the core purpose of onAnyMessage (fan-in from multiple channels) is untested.
findings.suggestion: Add a test that registers a channel with a controllable onMessage trigger, calls the trigger, and asserts the global handler receives the message.

findings.title: SlackChannel and WhatsAppChannel connect-without-deps error untested
findings.description: Both SlackChannel.connect() and WhatsAppChannel.connect() throw errors when called without injected dependencies, but no test verifies these error paths. These are distinct error paths -- one is a state guard, the other is a dependency configuration error.
findings.suggestion: Add tests for both channels: `new SlackChannel(config) // no deps` -> `await expect(channel.connect()).rejects.toThrow(/requires createApp/)` and similarly for WhatsApp.

findings.title: getByPlatform() leaks mutable ChannelRegistryEntry reference
findings.description: getByPlatform() returns the internal Map entry directly, allowing callers to mutate the channel, config, and registeredAt fields. This is the same mutable-reference-leak pattern previously flagged as P1 in SkillRegistry and CronService. listAll() similarly returns entries with mutable references.
findings.suggestion: Return a frozen snapshot or a readonly-typed copy: `getByPlatform(platform: ChannelPlatform): Readonly<ChannelRegistryEntry> | undefined`. Better yet, make ChannelRegistryEntry fields readonly in the interface definition and return a spread copy.

findings.title: register() does not validate channel.platform matches config.platform
findings.description: ChannelConfig is a discriminated union on `platform`, and Channel has readonly `platform`. But register() accepts any Channel + any ChannelConfig combination without verifying they agree on the platform discriminant. A caller could register a WhatsAppChannel with a SlackConfig.
findings.suggestion:
Add a runtime guard at the top of register(): `if (channel.platform !== config.platform) { throw new Error('Channel platform does not match config platform') }`. Alternatively, make register() generic: `register

(channel: Channel & { platform: P }, config: ChannelConfig & { platform: P })`.

findings.title: Unsafe `as` cast on external event data in WhatsApp handler
findings.code_snippet:
this.socket.ev.on('messages.upsert', (data: unknown) => {
  const event = data as BaileysUpsertEvent
findings.description: The baileys socket emits `unknown` typed data. The code casts it directly to BaileysUpsertEvent without any runtime validation. If baileys changes its event payload shape (it is a pre-release RC package), this will silently produce undefined property accesses or incorrect data rather than a clear error.
findings.suggestion: Add a runtime guard before casting: check that `data` is an object with a `messages` array property before proceeding.

findings.title: Unsafe `as` cast on Slack message data
findings.code_snippet: const slackMsg = message as SlackMessage
findings.description: The bolt message handler receives a message object that is cast directly to the local SlackMessage interface without runtime validation. Bolt's message event can contain various subtypes (message_changed, message_deleted, bot_message, etc.) that do not conform to SlackMessage shape.
findings.suggestion: Add a runtime guard: verify that `message` has the expected string properties (text, user, channel, ts) before processing.

findings.title: WhatsApp rate limit state stored only in memory
findings.code_snippet: private sentTimestamps: number[] = []
findings.description: The sentTimestamps array used for rate limiting is purely in-memory. If the process restarts, the rate limit resets immediately, potentially allowing a burst. For production use with multiple processes serving the same WhatsApp account, this provides no cross-process protection.
findings.suggestion: For production use, consider persisting rate limit state to a shared store (Redis/SQLite). At minimum, document that the rate limit is per-process and non-durable.

findings.title: No JSDoc on any exported type in types.ts
findings.code_snippet:
export type ChannelStatus = 'disconnected' | 'connecting' | 'connected' | 'error'
export interface Channel { ... }
findings.description: The types.ts file exports 9 public types with zero JSDoc documentation. The Channel interface has methods like sendMessage(target, content) where 'target' semantics vary by platform (Slack channel ID vs WhatsApp JID) -- this needs documentation.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc to each exported interface and type alias. At minimum, document Channel (the core contract), IncomingMessage (the canonical message shape), WhatsAppConfig/SlackConfig (config fields), and the target parameter semantics on Channel.sendMessage.

findings.title: No JSDoc on ChannelRegistry class or its public methods
findings.code_snippet: export class ChannelRegistry { ... register(), connectAll(), onAnyMessage() ... }
findings.description: ChannelRegistry has 7 public members -- all without any JSDoc. Key behaviors like 'register throws if platform already registered' and 'connectAll returns per-platform success/failure results' are non-obvious from signatures alone.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc to the class declaration and to each public method. Especially document: (1) register() throws on duplicate platform, (2) connectAll()/disconnectAll() return per-platform results and do not throw, (3) onAnyMessage() returns an unsubscribe function.

findings.title: No JSDoc on SlackChannel class or its public API
findings.code_snippet: export class SlackChannel implements Channel { ... }
findings.description: SlackChannel is an exported public class implementing the Channel interface. No JSDoc on the class, constructor, or any method. The dependency injection pattern via SlackChannelDeps is non-obvious and needs documentation.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc explaining it wraps @slack/bolt. Document the constructor's deps parameter and that sendMessage target is a Slack channel ID.

findings.title: No JSDoc on WhatsAppChannel class or its public API
findings.code_snippet: export class WhatsAppChannel implements Channel { ... }
findings.description: WhatsAppChannel has no JSDoc on the class, constructor, or methods. Rate limiting behavior and WhatsApp JID format for sendMessage target are not discoverable from the type signature alone.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc explaining it wraps @whiskeysockets/baileys. Document sendMessage target expects WhatsApp JID format and throws when rate limit is exceeded.

findings.title: Error messages lack operational context (channel ID, config)
findings.code_snippet: throw new Error('WhatsApp channel is not connected')
findings.description: Error messages in both channels lack operational context. 'Slack channel is not connected' and 'WhatsApp channel is not connected' do not include: the target ID being sent to, the current status value, or any config identifiers.
findings.suggestion: Include operational context: `throw new Error('WhatsApp sendMessage failed: channel status is ${this._status}, target: ${target}')`.

findings.title: connectAll and disconnectAll are near-identical (18 lines each)
findings.code_snippet: async connectAll() { ... } // identical to disconnectAll() except .connect() vs .disconnect()
findings.description: connectAll() and disconnectAll() share identical structure -- the only difference is calling entry.channel.connect() vs entry.channel.disconnect(). This is 18 lines duplicated nearly verbatim.
findings.suggestion: Extract a private `executeOnAll(operation)` helper. Both public methods become one-liners: `connectAll() { return this.executeOnAll(ch => ch.connect()) }`.

findings.title: Handler add/remove pattern duplicated 5 times across files
findings.description: The push-handler / return-unsubscribe-closure pattern appears 5 times across 3 files. Each instance is 5-6 lines of identical logic with only the array name changing.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared `addHandler<T>(handlers: T[], handler: T): () => void` utility. Each call site becomes a one-liner.

findings.title: setupEventHandlers has 4-level nesting depth
findings.description: setupEventHandlers contains 4 levels of nesting. Extracting a parseMessage() method would reduce nesting, separate parsing (pure logic) from event wiring (imperative shell), and make parsing independently testable.
findings.suggestion: Extract `parseMessage(msg): IncomingMessage | null` as a pure function. The event handler becomes: `for (const msg of event.messages) { const parsed = this.parseMessage(msg); if (parsed) { ... } }`.

findings.title: disconnectAll fault isolation not tested
findings.code_snippet: it('should disconnect all registered channels', async () => { ... })
findings.description: connectAll has a fault isolation test but disconnectAll has identical Promise.allSettled-style fault isolation logic with no corresponding test. If one channel throws during disconnect, the test suite does not verify other channels are still properly disconnected.
findings.suggestion: Add a test that registers a failing channel + a working channel, calls disconnectAll, and asserts the working channel still disconnected successfully.

findings.title: WhatsApp extendedTextMessage path untested
findings.code_snippet:
const text = msg.message.conversation
  ?? msg.message.extendedTextMessage?.text
  ?? ''
findings.description: All WhatsApp message tests only use the conversation path. The extendedTextMessage fallback (forwarded/quoted messages) is untested.
findings.suggestion: Add a test with extendedTextMessage format to verify the fallback path works correctly.

findings.title: WhatsApp message with no text content not tested
findings.code_snippet:
if (!msg.message) continue
const text = ... ?? ''
if (!text) continue
findings.description: The WhatsApp handler has guards for messages with no message property and messages where text resolves to empty string. Neither boundary condition is tested. Media-only messages (images, stickers) should be silently skipped.
findings.suggestion: Add boundary tests for media-only messages and messages with no message property.

findings.title: Slack message with empty text not tested
findings.code_snippet: if (!slackMsg.text) return
findings.description: The Slack message handler guards against messages with empty or undefined text, but no test covers this boundary condition. Slack sends events for message edits, deletions, and subtypes that may have no text field.
findings.suggestion: Add a boundary test triggering a message with empty text and asserting handlers are not called.

findings.title: ChannelStatus 'error' state never tested or transitioned to
findings.code_snippet: export type ChannelStatus = 'disconnected' | 'connecting' | 'connected' | 'error'
findings.description: The ChannelStatus type includes an 'error' state, but neither channel implementation ever transitions to it. When connect() fails, status is left in 'connecting'. No test verifies status after connection failure.
findings.suggestion: Add a test that verifies status after connection failure, then fix the implementation to set status to 'error' on failures.

findings.title: Unsafe `as BaileysUpsertEvent` cast on unknown event data
findings.code_snippet: const event = data as BaileysUpsertEvent
findings.description: The socket event handler receives `unknown` data and immediately casts it to BaileysUpsertEvent without validation. If the Baileys library changes its event shape, this cast will silently produce incorrect types.
findings.suggestion: Add a runtime type guard: `function isBaileysUpsertEvent(data: unknown): data is BaileysUpsertEvent { ... }` and validate before accessing fields.

findings.title: Unsafe `as SlackMessage` cast on Bolt message event
findings.code_snippet: const slackMsg = message as SlackMessage
findings.description: Bolt's message event can contain various subtypes (message_changed, message_deleted, bot_message, etc.) that do not conform to SlackMessage shape. The `text`, `user`, and `channel` fields may be undefined on certain subtypes.
findings.suggestion: Add a type guard to validate the message shape before processing: check that `text`, `user`, `channel`, and `ts` are present strings.

findings.title: IncomingMessage and ChannelRegistryEntry lack readonly modifiers
findings.description: IncomingMessage represents an immutable event -- once received, its fields should never change. All fields are mutable, allowing handlers to accidentally modify message properties as they propagate through the handler chain.
findings.suggestion: Add `readonly` to all IncomingMessage fields. For ChannelRegistryEntry, at minimum mark `config` and `registeredAt` as readonly.

findings.title: SlackChannel/WhatsAppChannel constructors accept config without validation
findings.description: Both channel constructors accept config objects without validating critical fields. SlackConfig's botToken and appToken could be empty strings. WhatsAppConfig's authDir could be empty.
findings.suggestion: Add constructor validation: `if (!config.botToken?.trim()) throw new Error('SlackConfig.botToken is required')`. Better: define Valibot schemas and validate at construction.

findings.title: Hardcoded test tokens in Slack test config
findings.code_snippet: botToken: process.env.SLACK_BOT_TOKEN ?? 'test-bot-token'
findings.description: Test file falls back to hardcoded strings 'test-bot-token' and 'test-app-token'. While not real secrets, the pattern normalizes token-like strings in test code.
findings.suggestion: Consider extracting test fixture configs to a shared test helper.

findings.title: connectAll/disconnectAll capture errors but callers must check each result
findings.description: connectAll() and disconnectAll() use a Result-like pattern which is good. However, the caller has no ergonomic way to know if ANY channel failed without iterating the entire map.
findings.suggestion: Consider adding an AggregateError throw when any failures occurred, or expose a `hasFailures(results)` helper.

findings.title: Slack connect() uses if/else where guard clause is cleaner
findings.description: The connect() method uses if/else to check deps. WhatsApp already uses the cleaner guard-clause pattern. Slack should match for consistency.
findings.suggestion: Flip to guard clause: `if (!this.deps) { throw ... } this.app = this.deps.createApp()`.

findings.title: ChannelOperationResult uses boolean+optional error instead of discriminated union
findings.description: This type allows illegal states: `{ success: true, error: new Error() }` and `{ success: false, error: undefined }`. A discriminated union would make these impossible.
findings.suggestion: Use: `type ChannelOperationResult = { success: true } | { success: false; error: Error }`.

findings.title: IncomingMessage.id is plain string across both platforms
findings.code_snippet: id: string
findings.description: IncomingMessage.id carries platform-specific semantics (Slack uses ts timestamp string, WhatsApp uses Baileys key.id). A branded type or documentation would prevent accidental cross-platform comparison.
findings.suggestion: Consider a branded type `MessageId = string & { readonly __brand: 'MessageId' }` or at minimum add JSDoc documenting the platform-specific format.

positive_observations: Clean Channel interface design with proper readonly properties and unsubscribe pattern (returns dispose function from onMessage/onStatusChange)
Discriminated union for ChannelConfig using platform literal types provides compile-time safety when adding new channel types
Test Double rationale comments properly documented for both Slack (@slack/bolt) and WhatsApp (@whiskeysockets/baileys) external service stubs
Fault isolation in ChannelRegistry.connectAll() -- one channel failure does not prevent others from connecting, with per-channel error results
Good test coverage: lifecycle, message dispatch, bot filtering, rate limiting, unsubscribe, pre-connect guard, and duplicate registration prevention
Zero forbidden patterns (TODO/FIXME/HACK/PLACEHOLDER/TEMP) found across all source files
Code is clean and self-documenting at the implementation level -- method names and parameter names are descriptive
connectAll/disconnectAll use per-channel error isolation with try/catch and Result objects -- prevents one failing channel from aborting others
ChannelOperationResult captures the actual Error object, not just a boolean, enabling callers to inspect failure causes
Error coercion pattern (error instanceof Error ? error : new Error(String(error))) handles non-Error throws correctly
All functions are short (under 30 lines), consistent with project conventions
Types are cleanly separated in types.ts with clear interface definitions
Guard clauses and early returns used well in WhatsApp channel (connect, setupEventHandlers)
Cyclomatic complexity per function stays in the 1-4 range (within project thresholds)
ChannelStatus and ChannelPlatform use literal unions instead of string enums -- consistent with codebase conventions
Channel interface exposes readonly platform and status with methods for mutation -- good encapsulation at the interface level
Dependency injection via constructor deps parameter enables testability without relying on module-level mocks
Both channels validate preconditions before I/O (not-connected check in sendMessage, deps check in connect)
WhatsApp rate limiting is implemented as a guard clause with clear error messaging
Test doubles for external APIs use dependency injection via constructor, making the test double boundary explicit and swappable
WhatsApp rate limiting is tested with a concrete scenario proving the boundary enforcement
Channel registry tests cover the full CRUD lifecycle: register, retrieve, list, unregister, and duplicate prevention
Both channel implementations test message handler unsubscription, ensuring no memory leaks from lingering handlers
Fault isolation for connectAll is properly tested, verifying one channel failure does not cascade to others
